metadata.summary: Design must serve both novice and expert users. Accelerators (keyboard shortcuts, batch operations, command palettes, customization) speed up expert workflows while remaining invisible to novices. A command palette is the single best surface for making accelerators discoverable.

content.title: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use
content.summary: Nielsen's Usability Heuristic #7: design must serve both novice and expert users. Accelerators (keyboard shortcuts, batch operations, command palettes, customization) speed up expert workflows while remaining invisible to novices. A command palette (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K) is the single best surface for making accelerators discoverable to both user types.

why.p: Interfaces designed only for novices frustrate power users who repeat the same actions daily. Interfaces designed only for experts overwhelm newcomers. The novice/expert spectrum is the most consistent source of usability complaints in productivity software; serving both requires layered affordances.

list.item: Auditing a feature spec or UI for missing keyboard shortcuts, batch operations, and power-user accelerators.
Reviewing shortcut schemes for consistency with platform conventions (Mac/Windows/web).
Identifying progressive disclosure opportunities: hiding advanced options without affecting novice users.
Code review: checking that CLI/API operations match UI operations (agent-accessibility parity).
Generating a Flexibility Audit report from an action inventory.
Product serves a single narrow use case with one user type — novice/expert design is unnecessary overhead.
Very early wireframing — flexibility patterns are implementation-level concerns, not concept-level.
Consumer mobile apps with infrequent use — most mobile users are perpetual novices; keyboard shortcuts do not apply.
When the bottleneck is latency, not interaction speed — shortcuts do not help if actions are slow.

section.name: user-spectrum
section.p: The interface must serve: novice (occasional users, need guided paths), intermediate (regular users, learning accelerators), power users (daily use, need shortcuts and automation). Design layers for all three simultaneously.

rule.statement: Implement a command palette (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K) as the single discoverability surface for all shortcuts.
rule.rationale: A command palette serves novice (can search for actions) and expert (can type known shortcut) users from the same UI surface; it is the most efficient single investment in flexibility.

section.name: keyboard-shortcuts

rule.statement: Show keyboard shortcuts in menu items and tooltips; shortcuts that are not discoverable do not exist for most users.
rule.rationale: Undiscoverable shortcuts are used only by users who read documentation; visible shortcuts in context menus teach users passively during normal use.

rule.statement: Never override Ctrl+W, Ctrl+N, Ctrl+T, or Ctrl+F in a web application.
rule.rationale: These browser-native shortcuts (close tab, new window, new tab, find) are deeply ingrained; overriding them creates severe friction and breaks platform expectations.

section.p: Layered accessibility: Level 1 = menu item (visible to all); Level 2 = keyboard shortcut shown in menu; Level 3 = expert knows by heart. All three levels from the same menu item.

example.title: Figma: Cmd+/ opens quick actions (command palette), showing all shortcuts with labels
example.note: New users discover shortcuts passively; experts use them daily.

example.title: Complex dashboard with no keyboard shortcuts, requiring mouse navigation for every action
example.note: Power users forced into slow click-only workflows daily.

rule.statement: Expose the 3-5 most-used settings immediately; place the remaining 80% behind an "Advanced" toggle.
rule.rationale: Showing all settings at once overwhelms novice users; hiding primary settings frustrates experts. The 80/20 split satisfies both.

rule.statement: For each action that affects a single item, design a batch variant as an accelerator layer.
rule.rationale: Single-item actions work for novices; batch operations unlock expert-level efficiency for high-volume workflows without adding complexity to the basic flow.

rule.statement: Follow platform shortcut conventions; validate agent shortcut recommendations against the specific framework and browser environment.
rule.rationale: Shortcut conflicts with browser/OS defaults (Ctrl+W, Ctrl+F) create severe friction; agent recommendations must be verified against target platform before implementation.

rule.statement: Compute segment results only for pre-specified segments — post-hoc shortcut adoption analysis inflates false positives.
rule.rationale: Shortcut usage varies by user type; analyzing only segments that look good post-hoc misrepresents adoption.

section.name: measuring-efficiency
section.p: Metrics to track: task completion time by user experience level, shortcut usage vs. mouse-click ratio per action, customization adoption rate (% who change defaults), error rate by user experience level, time on task over multiple sessions (learning curve slope).

rule.statement: Track keyboard vs. mouse event source per action before investing in additional shortcuts.
rule.rationale: If 95% of users use mouse for an action, keyboard shortcut investment has low ROI; data-driven shortcut prioritization is more effective than comprehensive coverage.

antipattern.description: Expert-only interface — all options always visible, no progressive disclosure.
antipattern.reason: Overwhelming novice users causes abandonment before they reach the point of becoming regular users; progressive disclosure is not optional for complex tools.

antipattern.description: Novice-only interface — no accelerators, every action requires full guided flow.
antipattern.reason: Power users who repeat the same guided flow dozens of times daily experience significant productivity loss and frustration.

antipattern.description: Over-customization — too many configuration options confuse users.
antipattern.reason: Customization beyond the 20% of settings users actually change adds cognitive load without benefit; limit customization to high-frequency actions and layout.

rule.statement: PM or lead must approve power-user feature recommendations before implementation; agent does not account for build complexity or support burden.
rule.rationale: Custom shortcuts, saved layouts, and command palettes have significant implementation cost; agent recommendations are feature ideas, not implementation decisions.

rule.statement: Filter agent-generated command palette action lists for internal or admin-only actions before exposing to end users.
rule.rationale: Agent may include operations from the full action inventory without distinguishing between user-facing and admin actions.

template.purpose: Bash script to extract all keyboard event listeners from a JS/TS codebase
